sites.name: Lausanne Cathedral (Cathédrale de Lausanne)
sites.description: One of the most beautiful Gothic cathedrals in Europe, completed in 1235 and dedicated to the Virgin Mary. Features include magnificent stained glass windows, a 13th-century rose window, and a 16th-century pipe organ. The 224 steps to the top of the belfry offer breathtaking panoramic views of the city, Lake Geneva, and the Alps.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round. The night watchman still calls out the hours from 10pm to 2am every night, continuing a tradition that has lasted over six centuries.
sites.booking_tips: No booking required. Located in the heart of the Old Town on a hilltop. The climb to the top of the belfry is worth it for the views but requires good physical condition.

sites.name: Olympic Museum (Musée Olympique)
sites.description: The world's largest information center on the Olympic Games, founded in 1993 by the International Olympic Committee. The museum showcases the history of the Olympics through interactive exhibits, historic artifacts, and multimedia displays including Olympic torches, medals, equipment, and memorabilia from ancient times to the present day.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open Tuesday to Sunday from 9am to 6pm, closed on Mondays except public holidays. Special exhibitions throughout the year.
sites.booking_tips: No advance booking required for individuals. Located in the Ouchy district near the lakefront. Allow at least 2 hours for the exhibition plus additional time for the Olympic Park and TOM Café with its panoramic lake views.

sites.name: Plateforme 10
sites.description: Lausanne's new arts district located near the train station, spanning 25,000 square meters. Houses three major museums: the Cantonal Museum of Fine Arts (MCBA), Photo Elysée (photography museum), and MUDAC (design and contemporary applied arts museum), alongside the Toms Pauli and Félix Vallotton foundations.
sites.seasonal_notes: MCBA is closed on Mondays, while Photo Elysée and MUDAC are closed on Tuesdays. Special exhibitions throughout the year.
sites.booking_tips: No booking required for general admission. Features restaurants, cafés, bookshops, and public spaces. Special events and exhibitions may require tickets in advance.

sites.name: Old Town of Lausanne (Vieille Ville)
sites.description: The charming medieval center of Lausanne with cobblestone streets, historic buildings, fountains, and squares. Key features include Place de la Palud with its animated clock, the steep Escaliers du Marché (wooden covered staircase from the 13th century), and numerous cafés, boutiques, and cultural sites.
sites.seasonal_notes: Beautiful year-round, especially lively during summer. Christmas markets in December. Farmers' market at Place de la Palud on Wednesdays and Saturdays.
sites.booking_tips: Best explored on foot. The terrain is quite hilly, so comfortable walking shoes are recommended. Free walking maps available at the tourist office. Consider guided walking tours for historical insights.

sites.name: Lake Geneva (Lac Léman)
sites.description: Western Europe's largest freshwater lake, forming a crescent between Switzerland and France with spectacular views of the Alps. Lausanne's lakefront features promenades, parks, and marinas, with opportunities for swimming, water sports, and boat cruises.
sites.price_range: Free to view, boat cruises vary
sites.seasonal_notes: Summer offers swimming and water activities. CGN boat cruises operate year-round but with reduced schedules in winter. Fall displays beautiful foliage colors and clear mountain views.
sites.booking_tips: The lakefront promenade is freely accessible. For boat trips on CGN vessels, check schedules as they vary by season. The most scenic cruises pass by the UNESCO-listed Lavaux vineyards between Lausanne and Montreux.

sites.name: Ouchy District
sites.description: The lakeside port area of Lausanne, once a fishing village, now a popular promenade with Belle Époque hotels, gardens, and the Olympic Museum. Features include Château d'Ouchy (a renovated 12th-century castle now a luxury hotel), boat docks, and numerous restaurants with lake views.
sites.seasonal_notes: Particularly vibrant during summer when the lakefront fills with outdoor dining and activities. Winter offers serene lake views and Alpine panoramas on clear days.
sites.booking_tips: Easily accessible via Lausanne's M2 metro line (Ouchy-Olympique station). Starting point for CGN boat cruises on Lake Geneva. Several hotels, restaurants, and ice cream shops in the area.

sites.name: Collection de l'Art Brut
sites.description: A unique museum dedicated to 'outsider art' created by self-taught artists often working outside conventional artistic traditions. Founded on the collection of French artist Jean Dubuffet, it showcases raw, unconventional, and visionary works by creators uninfluenced by mainstream cultural norms.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round with changing temporary exhibitions complementing the permanent collection.
sites.booking_tips: Located in the Beaulieu Castle, a short bus ride from the city center. The museum's content can be intense and unusual, offering a completely different perspective from traditional art museums.

sites.name: Sauvabelin Tower
sites.description: A wooden observation tower rising from the Sauvabelin Forest, constructed using environmentally sustainable methods. The spiral staircase leads to a platform offering 360-degree panoramic views of Lausanne, Lake Geneva, and the surrounding mountains.
sites.seasonal_notes: Accessible year-round, but views are best on clear days. The surrounding forest is particularly beautiful in autumn with colorful foliage.
sites.booking_tips: No booking required. Located in the Sauvabelin Forest park, which also features a small lake and farm animal enclosure, making it ideal for families. Accessible by public transport or a pleasant uphill walk from the city.

sites.name: Lavaux Vineyards
sites.description: UNESCO World Heritage terraced vineyards stretching along Lake Geneva's northern shore between Lausanne and Montreux. Dating back to the 11th century when Benedictine and Cistercian monasteries controlled the area, these steep hillside terraces produce primarily Chasselas white wine.
sites.seasonal_notes: Beautiful year-round but most vibrant from April to October. Harvest season (September-October) offers special events. The annual Caves Ouvertes (Open Cellars) in spring provides opportunities to visit numerous wineries.
sites.booking_tips: Accessible by train, with the Lavaux Express tourist train operating in season. Hiking trails connect vineyard villages like Lutry, Cully, and St-Saphorin. Wine tastings available at local cellars and during regional events.

sites.name: AQUATIS Aquarium-Vivarium
sites.description: Europe's largest freshwater aquarium and vivarium, showcasing ecosystems from five continents. Features include immersive exhibits on rivers and lakes worldwide, with thousands of fish, reptiles, and amphibians presented in naturalistic habitats focused on freshwater environments.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round. Indoor activity making it ideal for rainy days.
sites.booking_tips: Located north of the city center, accessible via the M2 metro (Vennes station). Advance booking recommended during peak tourist season and weekends. Adjacent to a large parking facility for those traveling by car.

sites.name: Fondation de l'Hermitage
sites.description: An art museum housed in a historic 19th-century mansion surrounded by beautiful gardens and offering views of the city. Presents high-quality temporary exhibitions of paintings, usually focused on 19th and 20th-century art, in an elegant, intimate setting.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round with changing exhibitions. Gardens particularly beautiful in spring and summer.
sites.booking_tips: Located uphill from the city center, accessible by bus or a scenic walk through the park. Check exhibition schedule before visiting as the museum focuses exclusively on temporary shows with no permanent collection on display.

sites.name: Rolex Learning Center
sites.description: An innovative architectural masterpiece on the EPFL (Swiss Federal Institute of Technology) campus designed by Japanese architects SANAA. The undulating one-story building houses a library, study spaces, cafés, and exhibition areas beneath a flowing concrete shell pierced by interior courtyards.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round with university schedule. Less crowded during university holidays.
sites.booking_tips: Accessible by metro M1 (EPFL station). Open to the public with the library accessible to visitors. Worth visiting for the extraordinary architecture alone, which has become a symbol of modern Lausanne.

sites.name: Musée de l'Elysée (Photo Elysée)
sites.description: One of Europe's foremost photography museums, now relocated to the Plateforme 10 arts district. Features a rich collection of over one million photographic images from the 19th century to the present, with changing exhibitions showcasing diverse aspects of photography as an art form.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round except Tuesdays. Changing exhibitions throughout the year.
sites.booking_tips: Now located at Plateforme 10 near the main train station. Combined tickets available with other museums in the arts district (MCBA and MUDAC).

sites.name: Lausanne Historical Museum (Musée Historique de Lausanne)
sites.description: Located in the former Bishop's Palace, this museum documents the city's rich history from prehistory to the present day. Exhibits include urban development models, artifacts, paintings, and multimedia presentations that trace how Lausanne evolved from a Roman settlement to a modern city.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round with rotating special exhibitions complementing the permanent collection.
sites.booking_tips: Located in the Old Town near the Cathedral. Provides excellent context for understanding the city before further exploration. Interactive elements make it suitable for families.

sites.name: Ouchy-Lausanne CGN Cruises
sites.description: Lake Geneva cruises departing from Lausanne-Ouchy port, offering various routes including scenic journeys along the UNESCO-listed Lavaux vineyards to Montreux and Chillon Castle. Options range from short panoramic excursions to gourmet dinner cruises aboard both modern vessels and historic Belle Époque paddle steamers.
sites.seasonal_notes: Services operate year-round but with reduced schedules in winter. Summer offers the fullest schedule with the most options. Historic paddle steamers operate primarily in high season.
sites.booking_tips: Departures from Lausanne-Ouchy port, accessible via M2 metro. Standard Swiss transportation discounts apply. Dinner and special themed cruises require advance reservations. The Lausanne-Montreux route offers the most spectacular scenery.

sites.name: Escaliers du Marché
sites.description: A picturesque covered wooden stairway built in the 13th century to connect the market at Place de la Palud with the Cathedral above. The stairs run alongside a row of medieval houses and shops, creating one of the most atmospheric routes through Lausanne's Old Town.
sites.seasonal_notes: Accessible year-round. Less crowded in early morning. Potentially slippery during winter months.
sites.booking_tips: No booking required. Several cafés with terraces along the route offer perfect spots to break the climb with refreshments. The stairs provide a direct route between the commercial center and the Cathedral.

sites.name: Parc de Mon Repos
sites.description: A historic public park with an elegant 19th-century mansion, manicured gardens, ancient trees, and an aviary. Located between the city center and the lake, it offers tranquil green space with classical landscaping, statues, and a small stream.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round. Most beautiful during spring when flowers bloom and in autumn when leaves change color.
sites.booking_tips: No booking required. Located halfway between the city center and Ouchy district. Perfect for a picnic or a relaxing break from sightseeing.

sites.name: Palais de Rumine
sites.description: An impressive Florentine Renaissance-style building housing several museums including natural history, geology, zoology, and archaeology collections. Built in 1906 and named after its benefactor Gabriel de Rumine, the palace also contains a library and university facilities.
sites.seasonal_notes: Individual museums have varying schedules, but most are open Tuesday through Sunday year-round.
sites.booking_tips: Located at Place de la Riponne in the city center. Housing multiple museums allows visitors to sample different collections based on interests. The building itself is architecturally significant and worth seeing even from the outside.

sites.name: Olympic Park
sites.description: The landscaped gardens surrounding the Olympic Museum, featuring sports-themed sculptures, Olympic symbols, and Mediterranean plantings. The terraced park stretches from the museum down to the Lake Geneva shoreline, with panoramic views across the water to the Alps.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round. Particularly pleasant in spring and summer when flowers are in bloom. The park is illuminated in the evening.
sites.booking_tips: No booking required. Accessible even when the museum is closed. Perfect for a stroll before or after visiting the Olympic Museum, or as a lakeside picnic spot with cultural elements.

sites.name: Château de Chillon
sites.description: Switzerland's most visited historic monument, a romantic medieval castle on a small island in Lake Geneva near Montreux. Dating from the 12th century, the fortress features original furnishings, medieval murals, subterranean vaults, and impressive state rooms that inspired Lord Byron's 'The Prisoner of Chillon.'
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round with extended hours in summer. Special events and medieval demonstrations on the first Sunday of each month.
sites.booking_tips: Located 30 minutes from Lausanne by train or lake cruise (the boat approach offers spectacular views). Audio guides available in multiple languages. Combined train-castle tickets offer savings.

neighborhoods.name: Ouchy
neighborhoods.character: Lausanne's picturesque lakefront district along Lake Geneva with a resort-like atmosphere, historic buildings, and recreational areas. Known for its beautiful promenades, parks, luxury hotels, and marina. The area offers stunning views of the Alps across the lake and serves as Lausanne's gateway to Lake Geneva with ferry connections to other lakeside towns.

location.description: Located on the shores of Lake Geneva (Lac Léman) at the southern edge of Lausanne, connected to the city center by the M2 metro line.

history.overview: Originally a fishing village separate from Lausanne, Ouchy developed into a fashionable resort area in the 19th century with the construction of luxury hotels and the arrival of wealthy travelers. The former Lausanne-Ouchy funicular railway (now replaced by the M2 metro) connected it to the city center, making it an integral part of the city while maintaining its distinct character.
history.significance: Represents Lausanne's connection to Lake Geneva and its development as a prestigious lakeside destination. The area has been the site of important historical events, including the signing of the Treaty of Lausanne in 1923 at the Beau-Rivage Palace Hotel.
notable_events: Development as a resort destination in the 19th century
Construction of the Lausanne-Ouchy funicular railway (1877)
Signing of the Treaty of Lausanne (1923)
Opening of the Olympic Museum (1993)
Replacement of the funicular with the M2 metro line (2008)

transit: M2 Metro line connects Ouchy to the city center and train station
CGN boat connections to other lakeside towns and cities in Switzerland and France
Bus lines serving the area
Limited parking available near the lake
Highly walkable lakefront promenade
practical_info.safety: Very safe area with good lighting and security, popular with tourists and locals at all hours.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Summer for lake activities and outdoor dining, sunset for spectacular views across the lake to the Alps, winter for peaceful lakeside walks.
practical_info.walkability: Excellent. The flat terrain along the lake makes it ideal for walking and cycling along designated paths.

known_for: Stunning views of Lake Geneva and the Alps

attractions.name: Olympic Museum
attractions.description: The world's largest archive of Olympic Games memorabilia spread across three floors, with interactive exhibits, historic Olympic torches, medals, equipment, and extensive multimedia displays celebrating Olympic history and values.
attractions.appeal: Interactive exhibits, beautiful grounds with sculptures, stunning lake views, Olympic history.

attractions.name: Ouchy Promenade
attractions.description: A picturesque tree-lined walkway along Lake Geneva with flower gardens, benches, sculptures, and panoramic views of the mountains and lake.
attractions.appeal: Scenic beauty, relaxation, people-watching, photography opportunities.

attractions.name: Château d'Ouchy
attractions.description: A neo-Gothic castle dating from the 12th century, rebuilt in the 19th century and now functioning as a luxury hotel with lakeside views and a historic tower.
attractions.appeal: Architectural beauty, historic significance, photo opportunities.

dining.name: Lakeside Restaurants
dining.known_for: Fresh lake fish, gourmet cuisine, waterfront terraces with Alpine views

dining.name: Hotel Restaurants
dining.known_for: Fine dining experiences in historic settings, Michelin-starred options

activities.name: Lake Cruises
activities.description: Take a scenic cruise on Lake Geneva aboard historic Belle Époque steamboats to nearby Swiss and French destinations.

activities.name: Water Sports
activities.description: Rent paddleboards, pedal boats, or take sailing lessons from providers along the lakefront.

stay_here_if: You want lakeside luxury and scenic views
You prefer flatter terrain without Lausanne's hills
You're interested in water activities and lake cruises
You want prestige accommodations with historic character
You value direct access to Lake Geneva
avoid_if: You're on a tight budget (area has many upscale hotels)
You want to be in the heart of the historic city center
You prefer a more local, less touristy experience
You're looking for vibrant nightlife
insider_tips: The lakefront can get busy on summer weekends - visit early morning for a more peaceful experience
Winter brings magic to Ouchy when the Alps across the lake are snow-covered and clearly visible
The gardens around the Olympic Museum are free to visit and offer excellent lake views
For the best sunset views, find a spot along the eastern section of the promenade

neighborhoods.name: Cité
neighborhoods.character: The historic heart of Lausanne perched on three hills, featuring medieval streets, the impressive Gothic cathedral, and historic buildings. This pedestrian-friendly district has a unique layout following the natural topography, creating a labyrinth of charming narrow streets, steep stairways, and hidden courtyards. The area combines historic patrimony with modern city life.

location.description: Located on a hill in the center of Lausanne, overlooking the lower parts of the city and Lake Geneva beyond.
landmarks: Place de la Palud and animated clock

history.overview: Established as the original settlement of Lausanne on defensible hilltops, the Cité developed from Roman times through the Middle Ages as a religious center around the Cathedral of Notre-Dame. The area was the administrative and religious heart of the city for centuries, with the Bishop of Lausanne holding power until the Protestant Reformation.
history.significance: Represents the historical origins of Lausanne and its religious importance. The cathedral was a major pilgrimage site in medieval Europe. The urban fabric demonstrates medieval city planning adapted to difficult terrain.
notable_events: Construction of the Cathedral (12th-13th centuries)
Construction of Château Saint-Maire (14th-15th centuries)
Protestant Reformation changing the city's religious character (16th century)
Urban renewals while preserving historic structures (19th-20th centuries)
Introduction of the night watchman tradition (continues today)

transit: M2 metro stops nearby (Riponne-Maurice Béjart, Bessières)
Limited vehicle access in many streets
Challenging for those with mobility issues due to steep inclines and stairs
Very little parking available within the district
practical_info.safety: Generally very safe, well-lit and with regular police patrols, though quiet at night in some sections.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Mornings for quieter exploration of the cathedral, Wednesdays and Saturdays for markets at Place de la Riponne and Place de la Palud.
practical_info.walkability: Highly walkable but strenuous due to steep hills and stairs. Comfortable shoes essential.

known_for: Lausanne Cathedral with its magnificent rose window
The night watchman who calls the hours after dark

attractions.name: Lausanne Cathedral
attractions.description: Switzerland's most impressive Gothic cathedral with a magnificent rose window, 13th-century painted portal, and an elevated position offering views across the city to Lake Geneva and the Alps.
attractions.appeal: Architectural majesty, historic importance, spectacular views from the tower.

attractions.name: Escaliers du Marché
attractions.description: A picturesque covered wooden stairway from the 13th century connecting the cathedral with the commercial lower town, lined with shops and cafes.
attractions.appeal: Unique architecture, atmospheric setting, historic significance.

attractions.name: Place de la Palud
attractions.description: Charming small square with a Renaissance fountain, the city hall, and an animated clock that performs a show every hour, hosting a market on Wednesdays and Saturdays.
attractions.appeal: Traditional city life, historic atmosphere, cultural performance of the clock.

dining.name: Historic Cafes
dining.known_for: Traditional settings in historic buildings, classic dishes, local wines

dining.name: Fine Dining Establishments
dining.known_for: Refined cuisine often in buildings with centuries of history

activities.name: Cathedral Tour
activities.description: Explore the magnificent Gothic cathedral, climb the tower for panoramic views, and see the famous rose window and painted portal.

activities.name: Night Watchman Experience
activities.description: Hear the night watchman call out the hours from the cathedral tower after dark, continuing a tradition from the Middle Ages.

stay_here_if: You want to immerse yourself in Lausanne's medieval history
You prefer charm and character over modern conveniences
You want to be at the historic heart of the city
You enjoy navigating atmospheric narrow streets and stairways
You're interested in religious architecture and history
avoid_if: You have mobility issues (many steep streets and stairs)
You need convenient parking nearby
You prefer modern accommodations with contemporary amenities
You're a light sleeper (cathedral bells and night watchman calls)
insider_tips: Visit the cathedral tower late afternoon for golden light on the lake views
Listen for the night watchman who calls the hours from 10 PM from the cathedral tower
Explore the small alleys branching from the main streets to find hidden courtyards and gardens
Saturday morning market at Place de la Riponne offers local products and a glimpse of local life

neighborhoods.name: Flon
neighborhoods.character: A former industrial zone transformed into Lausanne's trendiest district, featuring contemporary architecture, cultural venues, design shops, and vibrant nightlife. This pedestrian-friendly urban renewal success story combines restaurants, clubs, creative businesses, and public spaces in a distinctive grid layout that preserves the area's industrial heritage.

location.description: Located in a valley between the historic center and the Montbenon hill, easily accessible from the main train station.

history.overview: Originally a small river valley (the Flon river) that was covered and filled in the 19th century to create an industrial and warehouse district. After the decline of industry in the mid-20th century, the area became neglected until a major urban renewal project transformed it into a contemporary district for shopping, culture, and nightlife starting in the 1990s.
history.significance: Represents successful urban regeneration and the transformation of post-industrial spaces. Demonstrates Lausanne's evolution from an industrial past to a creative and cultural present.
notable_events: Covering of the Flon river and creation of industrial warehouses (19th century)
Industrial decline and period of neglect (mid-20th century)
Adoption of the renewal master plan (1990s)
Construction of the M2 and connection to Lausanne's metro system
Development as the city's premier entertainment and design district

transit: Major hub with M1 and M2 metro lines intersecting
Flon-Gare interface with multiple bus lines
Large underground parking garage
Pedestrianized central area
Mechanized walkways connecting to the train station
practical_info.safety: Generally safe with high foot traffic in evenings and good lighting, though can get rowdy on weekend nights due to the club scene.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Afternoons for shopping, evenings for dining and cultural events, weekend nights for clubbing and entertainment.
practical_info.walkability: Excellent within the district with mostly flat pedestrian zones, though reaching it from other parts of Lausanne might involve hills or stairs.

attractions.name: Place de l'Europe
attractions.description: The central plaza of the district used for events, exhibitions, seasonal attractions (ice rink in winter, outdoor seating in summer), surrounded by restaurants and shops.
attractions.appeal: Urban atmosphere, people-watching, events, central meeting point.

attractions.name: Les Arches
attractions.description: Historic railway viaduct arches repurposed into shops, bars, and restaurants creating a distinctive architectural landmark.
attractions.appeal: Unique repurposing of industrial heritage, atmospheric dining and drinking venues.

attractions.name: Public Art Installations
attractions.description: Rotating and permanent contemporary art pieces throughout the district, including sculptures, murals, and interactive installations.
attractions.appeal: Cultural interest, modern art, urban creativity, photo opportunities.

dining.name: Contemporary Restaurants
dining.known_for: Modern dining concepts, innovative cuisine, stylish settings

dining.name: Casual Eateries
dining.known_for: Quick, trendy food options ranging from gourmet burgers to world cuisines

activities.name: Nightlife
activities.description: Experience Lausanne's premier club scene with venues like MAD Club and various bars offering different music styles and atmospheres.

activities.name: Design Shopping
activities.description: Browse concept stores and designer boutiques featuring local and international brands, home décor, and unique gifts.

stay_here_if: You want to be at the center of Lausanne's nightlife
You appreciate contemporary design and architecture
You prefer centrally located accommodations with good transport connections
You enjoy a lively urban atmosphere
You want easy access to shopping and dining
avoid_if: You're a light sleeper (noise from clubs and bars, especially on weekends)
You prefer historic or traditional accommodations
You're seeking a quiet, residential neighborhood
You're traveling with very young children
insider_tips: Check the calendar for special events at Place de l'Europe which change seasonally
During summer, many venues have outdoor seating areas creating a festive atmosphere
The district transforms completely from day (shopping) to night (entertainment)
Some of the best views of the district are from the Grand Pont bridge above

neighborhoods.name: Sous-Gare
neighborhoods.character: A charming residential neighborhood located, as its name suggests, 'below the train station.' With tree-lined streets, elegant apartment buildings, and a relaxed atmosphere, this area offers a blend of local shops, neighborhood restaurants, and cultural venues. Popular with families and young professionals, it combines convenient location with authentic local character.

location.description: Located south of the main train station extending downhill toward Lake Geneva, between the station and Ouchy.

history.overview: Developed primarily in the late 19th and early 20th centuries following the construction of the Lausanne train station, which created a barrier between the upper and lower parts of the city. The neighborhood evolved as a desirable residential area for middle and upper-middle-class families, with fine examples of Art Nouveau and Belle Époque architecture.
history.significance: Illustrates the urban expansion of Lausanne during its period of growth and prosperity in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Represents the transition between the city center and the lakefront.
notable_events: Development following the construction of the train station
Construction of elegant apartment buildings in the Belle Époque era
Establishment as a desirable residential district
Recent gentrification and increasing popularity

transit: Multiple bus lines traversing the neighborhood
M2 metro line with several stops (Grancy, Délices, Jordils)
Walking distance to the main train station (uphill)
Street parking limited and often restricted to residents
practical_info.safety: Very safe residential area at all hours with good lighting and regular police patrols.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Weekday mornings for café culture, evenings for restaurant scene, weekends for relaxed neighborhood atmosphere.
practical_info.walkability: Good walkability with pleasant streets, though with some hill climbing required toward the station.

known_for: Proximity to both station and lake
best_for: Those wanting a convenient but non-touristy base
Those seeking a balance of accessibility and authenticity

attractions.name: Boulevard de Grancy
attractions.description: The heart of the district with tree-lined street, local shops, cafés with terraces, and authentic neighborhood atmosphere.
attractions.appeal: Local character, café culture, people-watching, architectural interest.

attractions.name: Milan Park
attractions.description: A charming neighborhood park with mature trees, play areas, and space for relaxation away from the bustle of the city.
attractions.appeal: Green space, local atmosphere, relaxation, family-friendly environment.

attractions.name: Délices Park
attractions.description: Small but elegant park with beautiful landscaping, benches, and space for contemplation.
attractions.appeal: Urban oasis, peaceful setting, neighborhood feeling.

dining.name: Neighborhood Bistros
dining.known_for: Authentic local cuisine, friendly service, non-touristy atmosphere

dining.name: International Restaurants
dining.known_for: Diverse offerings from Italian to Asian cuisines, neighborhood favorites

activities.name: Café Culture
activities.description: Experience local life at the neighborhood cafés where residents gather for coffee, pastries, and conversation.

activities.name: Architecture Walking
activities.description: Explore the streets to admire the elegant Art Nouveau and Belle Époque buildings that characterize the neighborhood.

stay_here_if: You want a convenient location with easy access to both city center and lakefront
You prefer an authentic residential atmosphere over touristy areas
You enjoy discovering local cafés, shops, and restaurants
You want to experience daily life in Lausanne
You value quiet nights but easy access to attractions
avoid_if: You want to be in the immediate vicinity of major tourist attractions
You prefer luxury accommodations (though there are some upper-end options)
You're looking for vibrant nightlife
You have difficulty with hills (some streets have significant inclines)
insider_tips: Some of Lausanne's best bakeries are found in this neighborhood - look for local favorites with queues in the morning
The route between the station and the lake through Sous-Gare is much more pleasant than the main Avenue d'Ouchy
Many restaurants in this area are closed on Sundays - plan accordingly
Locals often prefer the cafés here to the more touristy options in the center

neighborhoods.name: Montchoisi
neighborhoods.character: An elegant residential neighborhood known for its prestigious villas, beautiful gardens, and cultural institutions. Located on the hillside overlooking Lake Geneva, this peaceful district offers breathtaking views, tree-lined avenues, and a refined atmosphere. The area combines wealthy residential sections with notable museums and green spaces.

location.description: Located on the eastern side of Lausanne on the slopes leading down to Lake Geneva, approximately 2 km from the city center.

history.overview: Developed primarily in the 19th and early 20th centuries as a prestigious residential area for Lausanne's elite, featuring magnificent private estates and villas with lake views. Over time, some of these properties have been converted into museums, international organizations, and diplomatic residences.
history.significance: Represents the wealth and international character of Lausanne, as well as the city's cultural development through the repurposing of historic mansions into museums and institutions.
notable_events: Construction of elegant villas and estates in the 19th century
Establishment of the International Olympic Committee headquarters
Conversion of historic properties into cultural institutions
Creation of public parks from former private estates

transit: Bus lines connecting to the city center
Some distance from metro stations
Better parking options than in central Lausanne
Some steep streets making walking challenging
Taxi or private transportation recommended for those with mobility issues
practical_info.safety: Very safe upscale residential area with excellent security and minimal crime.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Spring and summer for gardens and parks, any time of year for museums and lake views.
practical_info.walkability: Moderate walkability with some steep streets, but pleasant for those able to navigate inclines.

known_for: Cultural museums (Photography Museum, Fondation de l'Hermitage)
best_for: Those seeking a quiet, upscale environment

attractions.name: Musée de l'Elysée
attractions.description: One of the world's leading museums dedicated solely to photography, housed in a beautiful 18th-century mansion with garden setting and lake views.
attractions.appeal: World-class photography exhibitions, historic setting, cultural significance.

attractions.name: Parc de Denantou
attractions.description: Elegant lakeside park featuring beautiful landscaping, the Thai Pavilion (a gift from the King of Thailand), rose gardens, and panoramic views of Lake Geneva and the Alps.
attractions.appeal: Natural beauty, cultural elements, relaxation, photography opportunities.

attractions.name: Olympic Committee Headquarters
attractions.description: The administrative headquarters of the International Olympic Committee with a small museum and Olympic memorabilia, set in a park-like environment.
attractions.appeal: Olympic history, architectural interest, international significance.

dining.name: Upscale Restaurants

dining.name: Museum Cafes
dining.known_for: Quality light meals and refreshments in cultural settings

activities.name: Museum Visits
activities.description: Explore the world-class photography collections at Musée de l'Elysée or art exhibitions at Fondation de l'Hermitage.

activities.name: Lakeside Gardens
activities.description: Stroll through the beautifully maintained parks and gardens along the lake with views of the Alps across the water.

Bellerive Plage is a beautiful public swimming complex for hot summer days
Look for the Thai Pavilion in Parc de Denantou, a gift from the King of Thailand and beautifully maintained
The walk between Ouchy and Montchoisi along the lake is one of Lausanne's most scenic routes

neighborhoods.name: Vidy
neighborhoods.character: A recreational paradise along Lake Geneva with extensive parks, sports facilities, Roman ruins, and beaches. This flat area offers a refreshing contrast to Lausanne's hills with open spaces, cycling paths, and recreational activities. Popular with families, sports enthusiasts, and those seeking outdoor relaxation.

location.description: Located along the southwest shore of Lake Geneva, extending west from Ouchy.

history.overview: Site of the ancient Roman settlement of Lousonna, established around 15 BCE, which gives Lausanne its name. After the Romans abandoned the site, it remained largely undeveloped until the 20th century when it became a recreational area. The 1964 Swiss National Exhibition held here transformed the area into parkland with facilities.
history.significance: Connects Lausanne to its Roman origins and represents the modern city's commitment to recreation, sports, and environmental preservation along the lakeshore.
notable_events: Roman settlement of Lousonna (15 BCE - 4th century CE)
Swiss National Exhibition (1964) transforming the area
Development of Olympic facilities and institutions
Creation of extensive parks and recreational areas

transit: Bus lines connecting to the city center
M1 metro line nearby for connection to university and city
Excellent cycling paths along the lake
Several parking areas available
Relatively flat terrain ideal for walking and cycling
practical_info.safety: Very safe area with good lighting on main paths, though some isolated areas of parkland should be avoided late at night.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Summer for beach activities and water sports, spring and autumn for pleasant walking and cycling, weekends for local atmosphere.
practical_info.walkability: Excellent walkability with flat terrain and numerous paths through parks and along the lake.

known_for: Lake Geneva beaches and swimming areas
best_for: History buffs interested in Roman history
Those seeking relaxation by the lake

attractions.name: Roman Ruins of Lousonna
attractions.description: Remains of the Roman settlement of Lousonna with a small museum displaying artifacts and explaining the history of Roman presence in the area.
attractions.appeal: Historical significance, archaeological interest, educational value.

attractions.name: Vidy Park and Beach
attractions.description: Expansive parkland with beaches, picnic areas, sports facilities, and walking paths stretching along Lake Geneva with views of the Alps.
attractions.appeal: Natural beauty, recreational opportunities, lake access, relaxation.

attractions.name: Olympic Institutions
attractions.description: Concentration of Olympic-related organizations including Swiss Olympic House and sports facilities reflecting Lausanne's status as Olympic Capital.
attractions.appeal: Sports history, international significance, architectural interest.

dining.name: Lakeside Restaurants
dining.known_for: Dining with lake views, seafood, casual meals in scenic settings

dining.name: Beach Cafes
dining.known_for: Light meals, snacks, and drinks in beachfront settings

activities.name: Water Sports
activities.description: Participate in paddleboarding, swimming, kayaking, and other water activities along the lakeshore.

activities.name: Cycling
activities.description: Ride along the well-maintained cycling paths that follow the lakeshore through parks and natural areas.
suitable_for: Those seeking easy exercise in scenic surroundings

stay_here_if: You prioritize access to outdoor recreation and lake activities
You prefer flat terrain rather than Lausanne's hills
You're traveling with children who need space to play
You enjoy swimming, cycling, or outdoor sports
You want a peaceful environment with natural beauty
avoid_if: You want to be in the center of urban activity
You prioritize access to shopping and nightlife

insider_tips: The restaurant at the Roman Museum has one of the most pleasant terraces in Lausanne
Early mornings at Vidy Beach offer beautiful light for photographers and peaceful swimming
The cycling path from Vidy to neighboring towns makes for an easy and scenic half-day excursion
Look for temporary art installations throughout the parks during summer months

neighborhoods.name: Sauvabelin
neighborhoods.character: A forested area on the heights above Lausanne offering natural beauty, outdoor recreation, and spectacular city views. Known for its lake, wooden observation tower, and animal park, this district provides a refreshing forest environment just minutes from the city center, making it popular with families, hikers, and those seeking nature and tranquility.

location.description: Located in the northern heights of Lausanne, approximately 2-3 km from the city center.

history.overview: Historically served as a forested region outside the city limits, used for logging and hunting. In the 19th century, it became a popular recreational area for Lausanne residents. The artificial lake was created in 1888 and the animal park established in the mid-20th century. The wooden tower was added in 2003 as an architectural project celebrating local timber construction.
history.significance: Represents Lausanne's connection to nature and the Swiss tradition of preserving natural spaces near urban centers. Demonstrates the city's commitment to providing green recreational spaces for residents.
notable_events: Creation of Sauvabelin Lake (1888)
Establishment of the animal park
Construction of the Sauvabelin Tower (2003)
Development of hiking trails and recreational facilities

transit: Bus lines connecting to the city center (particularly line 16)
No metro access
Limited parking available near the lake
Best reached by public transport or on foot from the city (uphill hike)
practical_info.safety: Very safe area during daylight hours; some isolated forest paths best avoided after dark.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: All seasons offer different charms: spring for wildflowers, summer for shade and lake activities, autumn for fall colors, winter for misty forest atmospheres.
practical_info.walkability: Excellent network of walking paths through the forest, though terrain can be uneven and hilly in places.

known_for: Wooden observation tower with panoramic views
Animal park with Swiss native species
Panoramic views of Lausanne, Lake Geneva, and the Alps
Natural environment close to the city
best_for: Those seeking escape from urban environment

attractions.name: Sauvabelin Tower
attractions.description: A 35-meter-high wooden observation tower built using traditional construction techniques without nails or screws, offering 360-degree views of the city, lake, and surrounding mountains.
attractions.appeal: Panoramic vistas, architectural interest, photographic opportunities.

attractions.name: Sauvabelin Lake
attractions.description: A small, picturesque artificial lake surrounded by forest, with rowboats for rent, a walking path around the perimeter, and a charming restaurant.
attractions.appeal: Scenic beauty, boating activities, peaceful atmosphere.

attractions.name: Sauvabelin Animal Park
attractions.description: A free, open-air mini-zoo featuring native Swiss animals such as deer, goats, pigs, and various bird species in a natural forest setting.
attractions.appeal: Family-friendly, educational, close encounters with animals.

dining.name: Chalet-Restaurant du Lac de Sauvabelin
dining.known_for: Traditional Swiss dishes served in a rustic chalet setting beside the lake

dining.name: Forest Cafes
dining.known_for: Basic refreshments and light meals in natural settings

activities.name: Forest Hiking
activities.description: Explore the network of marked trails through the Bois de Sauvabelin, offering various difficulty levels and distances.
suitable_for: Those seeking exercise in natural settings

activities.name: Rowboating
activities.description: Rent a rowboat to explore Sauvabelin Lake, a peaceful activity popular with families and couples.

stay_here_if: You prioritize natural surroundings and forest environment
You're willing to commute to the city center
You prefer quiet, non-urban accommodation
You're traveling with children who would enjoy the animal park and outdoor activities
You appreciate panoramic views and scenic beauty
avoid_if: You want immediate access to urban amenities
You dislike isolated locations
You prefer flat terrain (the area is hillside/forest)
You need frequent public transportation options
You're dependent on nearby shopping and services
insider_tips: Visit the tower early morning or near sunset for the most spectacular lighting and fewer visitors
The animal park is free of charge and particularly enjoyable for young children
There's a pleasant walking route from the city center up through the Hermitage grounds to Sauvabelin
The lake restaurant is popular on weekends - reservations recommended

neighborhoods.name: La Sallaz
neighborhoods.character: A rapidly developing district north of the city center that combines residential areas with commercial zones and excellent transport connections. Recently revitalized with the M2 metro extension, this neighborhood offers a mix of apartment complexes, shopping centers, and access to nearby forests and recreation areas. It serves as a gateway to northern Lausanne and surrounding communities.

location.description: Located north of the city center, on the upper slopes of Lausanne.
landmarks: La Sallaz metro station and transport hub

history.overview: Traditionally a transitional area between city and countryside that developed significantly in the post-WWII period with apartment buildings and commercial zones. The area underwent major redevelopment with the construction of the M2 metro line in the early 2000s, leading to new commercial centers and improved infrastructure.
history.significance: Illustrates Lausanne's urban expansion northward and the impact of modern transportation infrastructure on neighborhood development. Shows the evolution from peripheral area to integrated urban district.
notable_events: Post-war residential development
Construction of the M2 metro line with La Sallaz station
Major urban renewal projects in the 2000s-2010s
Development of Biopôle research campus nearby

transit: Major transport hub with M2 metro station
Multiple bus lines intersection
Park and Ride facilities
Good road connections to highways
Gateway to northern suburbs
practical_info.safety: Generally safe area with good lighting around the commercial and transport centers.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Weekdays for normal local atmosphere, avoid rush hours if using the transport hub.
practical_info.walkability: Variable walkability with some steep sections, good pedestrian infrastructure near the metro and commercial areas.

known_for: Access to northern forests and recreational areas
Proximity to CHUV hospital and medical centers
Gateway to the upper parts of Lausanne
best_for: Visitors to CHUV hospital or Biopôle
Those preferring modern facilities over historic charm

attractions.name: Aquatis
attractions.description: Europe's largest freshwater aquarium, showcasing ecosystems from around the world with innovative displays, interactive exhibits, and educational presentations.
attractions.appeal: Family-friendly, educational value, unique aquatic exhibits, rainy day activity.

attractions.name: AquaSplash
attractions.description: Indoor water park with slides, wave pools, and recreational swimming facilities suitable for all ages.
attractions.appeal: Family entertainment, all-weather activity, recreational fun.

attractions.name: Biopôle
attractions.description: Life sciences campus hosting research institutions and biotech companies in a modern architectural setting.
attractions.appeal: Architectural interest, scientific significance, contemporary design.

dining.name: Shopping Center Restaurants
dining.known_for: Convenient dining options, chain restaurants, diverse food choices

dining.name: Local Cafes
dining.known_for: Quick meals, coffee, meeting places for residents and workers

activities.name: Aquatis Visit
activities.description: Explore the extensive freshwater aquarium with exhibits featuring ecosystems from five continents.

activities.name: Forest Access
activities.description: Use La Sallaz as a starting point for hikes into the northern forests and countryside.
suitable_for: Visitors wanting to explore beyond the city

avoid_if: You want historic Swiss atmosphere

You're seeking picturesque or tourist-oriented areas
You want to be close to nightlife and cultural activities
insider_tips: The M2 metro from La Sallaz offers one of the most scenic urban transport routes as it descends toward the lake
Aquatis is much more than just an aquarium - plan at least half a day for a proper visit
The forest paths beginning just beyond La Sallaz lead to beautiful countryside within minutes
The Park and Ride facilities make this a good base if you're exploring the region by car

neighborhoods.name: Chailly
neighborhoods.character: A pleasant residential district on the eastern side of Lausanne that maintains a village-like atmosphere despite being fully integrated into the city. With a mix of apartment buildings, single-family homes, and some older farm buildings, this neighborhood offers a quieter lifestyle with good amenities. The area balances urban convenience with residential tranquility.

location.description: Located on the eastern plateau of Lausanne, between the city center and Pully.

history.overview: Originally a separate village outside Lausanne that gradually became incorporated into the expanding city during the 20th century. The area has retained elements of its village character and historical buildings while integrating modern residential and commercial development.
history.significance: Represents the absorption of surrounding villages into greater Lausanne while demonstrating how neighborhoods can maintain distinct identities and community feeling within a larger urban context.
notable_events: Existence as an independent village before merging with Lausanne
Preservation of village center with some historic buildings
Development of modern residential areas while maintaining green spaces
Integration into Lausanne's urban fabric while preserving local character

transit: Several bus lines connecting to the city center
No direct metro access (nearest stations in adjacent neighborhoods)
Better parking options than in central Lausanne
Cycling routes to center and lake
practical_info.safety: Very safe residential area with strong community presence and good lighting.
practical_info.best_time_to_visit: Weekday mornings for local market atmosphere, year-round for residential charm.
practical_info.walkability: Good walkability within the neighborhood, though some distances to major attractions and hilly in sections.

known_for: Blend of old and new architecture
Proximity to both center and countryside
best_for: Those wanting local character without tourist crowds
Those who prefer residential calm but with good connections

attractions.name: Chailly Village Center
attractions.description: The heart of the former village with some historic buildings, local shops, a church, and community atmosphere.
attractions.appeal: Authentic local life, architectural interest, community feeling.

attractions.name: La Vuachère Valley
attractions.description: A small river valley with walking paths, forests, and natural areas cutting through the eastern part of Lausanne.
attractions.appeal: Natural beauty, walking opportunities, escape from urban environment.

attractions.name: Parc de Valency
attractions.description: Large park with mature trees, playgrounds, walking paths, and open spaces for recreation.
attractions.appeal: Green space, family activities, relaxation, local atmosphere.

dining.name: Neighborhood Restaurants
dining.known_for: Local favorites serving residents rather than tourists, authentic cuisine

dining.name: Local Bakeries and Cafes
dining.known_for: Morning pastries, coffee, casual meals in neighborhood settings

activities.name: Park Visits
activities.description: Enjoy the neighborhood parks and green spaces popular with local families and residents.

You prefer living like a local rather than a tourist
